_purpose: County Landscape Profile for Hoke County, NC. Formed from Robeson County in 1911. Northeastern edge of the Lumbee corridor, where the Sandhills meet the Coastal Plain. Fort Bragg's expansion after 1918 transformed the western portion of the county.
display_name: Hoke County, North Carolina — Landscape Profile

physical_geography.physiographic_province: Atlantic Coastal Plain — transitional zone between the Sandhills and the Inner Coastal Plain. The western portion is Sandhills terrain (deep sand, longleaf pine remnants); the eastern portion grades into the flatter Coastal Plain along the Lumber River tributaries.
physical_geography.elevation_range: 150-350 ft. Higher and hillier than Robeson County; the Sandhills influence is pronounced on the western side near the Cumberland County line.
physical_geography.dominant_terrain: Rolling sandy hills in the west (Sandhills), grading into flatter sandy loam in the east along tributaries of the Lumber River. The terrain reflects the county's position as a transitional zone — too sandy for prime agriculture in the west, better farmland toward the Lumber River in the east. Rockfish Creek, a significant tributary, drains southward through the county. The western portion was largely absorbed by Fort Bragg's expansion in the 1940s, permanently removing thousands of acres from agricultural use.

soil_types.description: Deep, excessively drained sands on the Sandhills ridges in western Hoke County. White to yellowish sand, several feet deep, with minimal organic content.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Nearly useless for agriculture without heavy fertilization and irrigation. Supported longleaf pine but not row crops. This land became part of Fort Bragg's reservation — the Army wanted it precisely because it was too poor to farm.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, NC (1921; rev. 1984).

soil_types.description: Well-drained sandy loam in the eastern and central portions, particularly along Rockfish Creek and the tributaries flowing toward the Lumber River.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: The county's best farmland. Supported tobacco and cotton, though in smaller acreages than Robeson County. Lumbee families in Hoke County concentrated on Norfolk loam along the eastern river corridors.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, NC (1921; rev. 1984).

soil_types.description: Poorly drained alluvial and organic soils along Rockfish Creek and Lumber River tributaries.
soil_types.agricultural_impact: Too wet for row crops. Provided timber and the same refuge function found in Robeson County's swamps, though the swamp systems in Hoke County were smaller and less extensive.
soil_types.source: USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, NC (1921; rev. 1984).

major_waterways.name: Rockfish Creek
major_waterways.role: Primary drainage, settlement corridor, mill sites
major_waterways.description: A significant tributary flowing south through Hoke County toward the Lumber River. Rockfish Creek powered gristmills and sawmills in the nineteenth century and defined the settlement corridor for both Lumbee and white families. The creek's name reflects the fish populations that supplemented local diet.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: Rockfish Creek was {ancestor_name}'s nearest water — a sand-bottomed stream that powered the gristmill, watered the mule, and provided the catfish and bream that supplemented what the sandy soil would not grow.

major_waterways.name: Lumber River tributaries (eastern Hoke)
major_waterways.description: Small streams draining eastward into the Lumber River system connected Hoke County's Lumbee communities to the larger homeland in Robeson County. These tributaries carried the same blackwater character as the Lumber River itself.
major_waterways.narrative_hook: The streams that ran east from {ancestor_name}'s land in Hoke County drained into the Lumber River — the same dark water that connected the Lumbee communities from Scotland County to Columbus, a homeland mapped by waterways rather than county lines.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Longleaf pine savanna (exceptionally well-developed on the Sandhills side); bottomland hardwoods
vegetation_eras.description: Hoke County's western Sandhills supported some of the finest longleaf pine forest in the Southeast — tall, widely spaced trees in deep sand, maintained by fire. The eastern portion carried the same longleaf-bottomland pattern as Robeson County.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Turpentine-worked and lumber-cut longleaf declining; farm clearings expanding on better soils
vegetation_eras.description: The area was part of Robeson County until 1911. Naval stores and lumber industries stripped the longleaf. The Sandhills longleaf was cut for lumber rather than boxed for turpentine — the deep sand supported tall, straight timber trees valued for masts and construction. Farm clearings expanded on the Norfolk loam in the east.

vegetation_eras.dominant_cover: Mixed agriculture on eastern soils; military reservation on western Sandhills; loblolly second-growth
vegetation_eras.description: After county formation in 1911, agriculture dominated the eastern half. Fort Bragg's establishment in 1918 and massive WWII expansion absorbed the western Sandhills — the Army's need for artillery training ranges matched the landscape's unsuitability for farming. The military reservation preserved some of the best remaining longleaf pine forest in the state, an ironic conservation outcome.
vegetation_eras.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985); Frost, 'Longleaf Pine Ecosystem' (2006).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Hoke County in summer was two landscapes stitched together. In the east, along Rockfish Creek, the Norfolk loam grew tobacco and the curing barns trailed their thin smoke into a humid sky — the same sweet, heavy smell as Robeson County, the same bent-back labor in the rows. A mile to the west, the Sandhills began: deep white sand that would not hold a footprint, scattered longleaf pines casting long shadows, and a silence broken only by the rattlesnake-buzz of cicadas. Between these two worlds, Raeford — the tiny county seat — sat at the crossroads, barely distinguishable from a dozen other coastal plain villages. The artillery at Fort Bragg, fifteen miles to the northwest, boomed on training days, a sound that carried across the flat country and reminded everyone that the Army had arrived.
sensory_snapshots.source: USDA Soil Survey (1921); Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: By the spring of 1942, the western third of Hoke County had vanished into Fort Bragg. Families who had farmed the sandy ridges for generations received notices and small payments and moved east, crowding into the remaining agricultural land along Rockfish Creek. The roads that had been sandy farm tracks were paved with military concrete, and convoys of Army trucks raised dust clouds visible for miles. In the Lumbee communities east of Raeford, the spring tobacco work went on — plant beds burning in the cold March air, seedlings going into the ground in April rain — but the young men were disappearing into uniforms, and the artillery practice from the west never stopped.
sensory_snapshots.source: Daniel, Breaking the Land (1985); Lowery, Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South (2010).

sensory_snapshots.snapshot: Before it was Hoke County, this was the northern edge of Robeson — and in autumn the turpentine camps were shutting down for the season while the cotton fields whitened and the first tobacco went to market by mule wagon to Lumberton. The longleaf pines on the sandy ridges were mostly scarred or dead by the 1890s, their turpentine faces blackened and oozing the last of their resin. The air smelled of pine and woodsmoke and the dry-leaf smell of autumn. At the crossroads that would become Raeford, the general store was the center of everything — news, credit, and the merchant's ledger that held every farmer's debt.
sensory_snapshots.source: Outland, Tapping the Pines (2004); USDA Soil Survey (1921).

landscape_changes.change: County formation — Hoke carved from Robeson
landscape_changes.description: Hoke County was created in 1911 from the northern portion of Robeson County. Raeford became the county seat. The new county split Lumbee communities that had been in a single jurisdiction, creating a new courthouse with new records beginning in 1911. Earlier records for Hoke County families are filed in Robeson County.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: County formation means a records boundary at 1911. Tracing families across this date requires checking both Hoke and Robeson County courthouses.
landscape_changes.source: Lefler and Newsome, North Carolina: The History of a Southern State (1973).

landscape_changes.description: Fort Bragg was established in 1918 as a WWI artillery training camp on the Sandhills in Cumberland and Hoke counties. The WWII expansion (1940-1942) massively enlarged the reservation, absorbing the western third of Hoke County. Families were displaced. Roads were built. The military economy that transformed Cumberland County's Fayetteville also reshaped Hoke County — though Raeford remained small, it gained spillover military traffic and employment.
landscape_changes.narrative_impact: The Fort Bragg expansion displaced families from land they had farmed for generations. It also provided wage employment that had never existed in this agricultural backwater. Both effects shaped migration decisions for Lumbee families.
landscape_changes.source: U.S. Army Center of Military History. Fort Bragg installation history.

narrative_hooks: Hoke County was born in 1911, carved from the top of Robeson County — but {ancestor_name}'s family had been farming this land since long before anyone drew that line, and the new courthouse in Raeford did not change who they were or where they belonged.

When Fort Bragg swallowed the western ridges in {year}, {ancestor_name} watched artillery ranges replace the longleaf pines — the same pines {pronoun_possessive} grandfather had tapped for turpentine, the same sand ridges where the Lumbee had hunted for centuries.

The Sandhills soil west of Raeford was too poor to farm and too dry to hold a crop — which is exactly why the Army wanted it, and exactly why {ancestor_name}'s family farmed the better loam along Rockfish Creek instead.
By {year}, the boom of Fort Bragg's artillery was a daily sound in Hoke County — a reminder that the world had found even this quiet corner of the Lumbee corridor.
source_references: Daniel, Pete. Breaking the Land: The Transformation of Cotton, Tobacco, and Rice Cultures since 1880. Urbana: University of Illinois Press, 1985.
Outland, Robert B., III. Tapping the Pines: The Naval Stores Industry in the American South. Baton Rouge: Louisiana State University Press, 2004.
Frost, Cecil C. 'History and Future of the Longleaf Pine Ecosystem.' In The Longleaf Pine Ecosystem, ed. Jose et al., 9-48. New York: Springer, 2006.
Lowery, Malinda Maynor. Lumbee Indians in the Jim Crow South. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 2010.
Lefler, H.T., and A.R. Newsome. North Carolina: The History of a Southern State. 3rd ed. Chapel Hill: University of North Carolina Press, 1973.
USDA Soil Survey of Hoke County, North Carolina. Washington, DC: Government Printing Office, 1921; revised 1984.
